candidates.name: Technical Debt
candidates.description: Ward Cunningham's financial metaphor: shortcuts accrue interest, must be paid down. One of the most structurally rich metaphors in software -- principal, interest, bankruptcy all map.

candidates.name: Code Smell
candidates.description: Martin Fowler/Kent Beck's olfactory metaphor: bad code is detected by intuitive 'smell' before rational analysis. Maps the pre-conscious pattern recognition of bodily senses onto code review.

candidates.name: Spaghetti Code
candidates.description: Tangled pasta maps onto tangled control flow. Part of a food-metaphor family: lasagna code (too many layers), ravioli code (well-encapsulated), baklava code (excessive thin layers).

candidates.name: Rubber Duck Debugging
candidates.description: Explaining code to an inanimate listener forces articulation that reveals bugs. The duck as interlocutor maps social cognition onto individual problem-solving.

candidates.name: Yak Shaving
candidates.description: An absurd prerequisite chain (needing to shave a yak before you can start your real task). Maps the feeling of infinite regression in dependency resolution.

candidates.name: Bikeshedding
candidates.description: Parkinson's Law of Triviality: a committee spends more time on the bike shed color than the nuclear reactor. Maps trivial accessibility onto disproportionate attention.

candidates.name: Cargo Cult Programming
candidates.description: Melanesian cargo cults built ritual airstrips hoping planes would return. Maps ritual imitation without understanding onto copying code patterns without comprehension.

candidates.name: Heisenbug
candidates.description: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle mapped onto bugs that disappear under observation. Part of a physics-bug family: Bohrbug (deterministic), Mandelbug (chaotic), Schrodinbug (exists only once observed).

candidates.name: Big Ball of Mud
candidates.description: A shapeless, structureless mass maps onto software that grew without architecture. Brian Foote and Joseph Yoder's 1997 paper named the most common architecture of all.

candidates.name: Software Rot
candidates.description: Organic decay maps onto code degradation. Also called bit rot, code rot, software entropy. The metaphor imports inevitability and the need for maintenance as hygiene.

candidates.name: Boat Anchor
candidates.description: Dead weight dragging you down maps onto obsolete technology kept in the codebase. Nobody dares remove it because it might be load-bearing.

candidates.name: Dogfooding
candidates.description: Eating your own dog food: using your own product internally. Maps the trust test (would you feed this to your own pet?) onto product quality validation.

candidates.name: Bus Factor
candidates.description: How many people could get hit by a bus before the project dies? Maps violent accident onto knowledge concentration risk. The morbidity makes the metric memorable.

candidates.name: Hydra Code
candidates.description: The Lernaean Hydra: cut one head, two grow back. Maps mythological regeneration onto bugs that multiply when fixed. The only solution is to rewrite (cauterize).

candidates.name: Jenga Code
candidates.description: A precarious block tower where removing any piece risks collapse. Maps structural fragility and interdependence onto tightly coupled codebases.

candidates.name: Shotgun Debugging
candidates.description: Firing a shotgun (wide spread, low precision) maps onto making random changes hoping to hit the bug. Contrasts with surgical/sniper debugging.

candidates.name: Deep Magic
candidates.description: Arcane mystical knowledge maps onto obscure technical expertise. From the Jargon File. 'Here be dragons' territory -- code that works but nobody understands why.

candidates.name: Baklava Code
candidates.description: Pastry with too many thin layers maps onto excessive abstraction layers. Each layer adds overhead, the filling (business logic) gets lost between the phyllo (interfaces).

candidates.name: Golden Hammer
candidates.description: Maslow's hammer: if all you have is a hammer, everything looks like a nail. Maps tool fixation onto technology choice bias. The 'golden' adds the seductive appeal of the familiar.

candidates.name: God Object
candidates.description: An omniscient, omnipotent deity maps onto a class that knows too much and does too much. The theological metaphor reveals the anti-pattern: no single entity should be omnipotent.

candidates.name: Lava Flow
candidates.description: Volcanic lava that hardens into immovable rock maps onto dead code that solidifies in the codebase. Once cooled, nobody dares touch it.

candidates.name: Duck Typing
candidates.description: If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it is a duck. Maps behavioral identity (pragmatist epistemology) onto type systems. The duck test as interface contract.

candidates.name: Monkey Patching
candidates.description: Mischievous monkey tampering maps onto runtime modification of code you don't own. Originally 'guerrilla patching' -- the animal metaphor stuck because it captures the recklessness.

candidates.name: Zombie Process
candidates.description: The undead: a process that has terminated but still occupies the process table. Maps horror-genre reanimation onto resource leaks. Parent must 'reap' the zombie (funerary metaphor on top of horror metaphor).

candidates.name: Orphan Process
candidates.description: A child whose parent has died. Maps familial abandonment onto processes whose parent terminated without cleanup. The init process 'adopts' orphans -- the metaphor is internally consistent and poignant.

candidates.name: Race Condition
candidates.description: A footrace where the outcome depends on who arrives first. Maps competitive timing onto concurrent access bugs. So embedded in CS that most forget it is a metaphor.

candidates.name: Magic Number
candidates.description: Unexplained constants in code are 'magic' -- they work but nobody knows why. Maps supernatural causation onto opaque implementation details. The fix is to name them, breaking the spell.

candidates.name: Garbage Collection
candidates.description: Municipal waste collection maps onto automatic memory reclamation. Trash trucks, garbage cans, recycling -- the entire sanitation infrastructure metaphor carries over. So dead most developers never notice.

candidates.name: Sandbox
candidates.description: A children's sandbox: a safe, bounded space for play. Maps the concept of a consequence-free enclosure onto isolated execution environments. You can build and destroy without affecting the real world.

candidates.name: Breadcrumb Trail
candidates.description: Hansel and Gretel's trail of breadcrumbs maps onto debug logging and UI navigation paths. Leave a trail so you can find your way back -- or so others can follow your path.

candidates.name: Skunkworks
candidates.description: Lockheed Martin's secret Advanced Development Programs division. Maps autonomous military R&D onto small teams given freedom to innovate outside normal organizational constraints.

candidates.name: Drinking the Kool-Aid
candidates.description: Jonestown mass poisoning maps onto uncritical adoption of a technology, methodology, or corporate culture. The darkest origin story of any tech metaphor.

candidates.name: Boil the Ocean
candidates.description: An impossible physical task maps onto overambitious project scope. You cannot boil the ocean; you should not try to build everything at once.

candidates.name: Accidental Complexity vs Essential Complexity
candidates.description: Fred Brooks borrowed Aristotle's essential/accidental distinction. Maps philosophical ontology onto software engineering: some complexity is inherent in the problem, some is self-inflicted by tools and process.

candidates.name: Silver Bullet
candidates.description: The only weapon that kills a werewolf. Fred Brooks: 'there is no silver bullet' in software engineering. Maps supernatural monster-slaying onto the search for a single solution to software complexity.

candidates.name: Technical Bankruptcy
candidates.description: Extension of the technical debt metaphor: when debt accumulates beyond repayment capacity, you declare bankruptcy (total rewrite). Maps financial insolvency onto the rewrite-vs-refactor decision.

candidates.name: Fear Driven Development
candidates.description: Management through fear of firing, deadline crunch, or resource cuts. Maps coercive psychology onto development methodology. A dark mirror of test-driven, behavior-driven, etc.

candidates.name: Yo-Yo Problem
candidates.description: A toy that goes up and down on a string maps onto navigating deep class inheritance hierarchies. You bounce between parent and child classes trying to understand behavior.

candidates.name: Action at a Distance
candidates.description: Einstein's 'spooky action at a distance' in quantum physics maps onto code where modifying one part unexpectedly affects distant, seemingly unrelated parts. Global state and hidden coupling.

candidates.name: Software Peter Principle
candidates.description: The Peter Principle (people rise to their level of incompetence) mapped onto software: projects grow in complexity until they exceed even the original developers' comprehension.

candidates.name: Voodoo Programming
candidates.description: Voodoo ritual (manipulating effigies to affect the real world) maps onto trial-and-error programming where the developer doesn't understand the causal mechanism.
